title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The kubelet.conf file is the kubeconfig file for the node, and controls various parameters that set the behavior and identity of the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the following command to check the permissions of the kubelet.conf on each node: for node in $(oc get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}') do oc debug node/${node} -- chroot /host stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf done Verify that the permissions are 644 .
input_row.default_value: By default, OpenShift sets the default permissions for the kubelet.conf to 644 .

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be fully verified via IBM Cloud APIs/IBM Cloud CLI.
- The audit mentions commands that can be translated into equivalent CLI/SDK methods.
- IBM Cloud infrastructure supports querying configurations associated with Kubernetes resources, which includes checks on file permissions like `kubelet.conf`, leveraging the OpenShift APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_openshift_node_kubelet_conf_permissions_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the command `oc get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'` to enumerate all nodes in the OpenShift cluster.
- For each node, use the command `oc debug node/{node} -- chroot /host stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` to check the permissions of the `kubelet.conf` file.
- Validate that the permissions retrieved should be `644` or more restrictive (i.e., less than `644` means the file is too permissive).
- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: The permissions are `644` or more restrictive
  - Fail: The permissions are anything more permissive than `644` (like `777`, `755`, etc.) or the file does not exist as expected.

This automated approach enables efficient verification of the compliance control while leveraging the inherent capabilities of IBM Cloud and OpenShift APIs.
